title: Height above sea level

infobox.key: [Authority control databases](/wiki/Help:Authority_control "Help:Authority control"): National [Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q6452016#identifiers "Edit this at Wikidata")

infobox.value: is a measure of a location's [vertical distance](/wiki/Vertical_position "Vertical position") ([height](/wiki/Height "Height"), [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") or [altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude")) in reference to a [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") based on a historic [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level"). In [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy"), it is formalized as [orthometric height](/wiki/Orthometric_height "Orthometric height"). The zero level varies in different countries due to different reference points and historic measurement periods. [Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and [other forces](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence") can cause sea levels and elevations to vary over time.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

["Sea Level: Frequently Asked Questions and Answers"](https://psmsl.org/train_and_info/faqs/). Liverpool, UK: [National Oceanography Centre](/wiki/National_Oceanography_Centre "National Oceanography Centre"). Retrieved 31 January 2024.
["Altitude, Flight Level and Height"](https://skybrary.aero/articles/altitude-flight-level-and-height). *SKYbrary Aviation Safety*. Retrieved 31 January 2024.

["TOWAIR Determination"](https://wireless2.fcc.gov/helpfiles/AsrHelp/help-towairdetermination.html). Federal Communications Commission. Retrieved 3 February 2024. "Site Elevation (AMSL) – The elevation of the site above mean sea level (AMSL)."

Press Office (30 April 2021). ["Ordnance Datum Newlyn - 100 years old"](https://www.ordnancesurvey.co.uk/blog/ordnance-datum-newlyn-100-years-old). *[Ordnance Survey](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey "Ordnance Survey")*.

["General Concepts"](https://laartcc.org/stm/general-concepts). Los Angeles Air Route Traffic Control Center. Retrieved 6 April 2024. "Sometimes, you will still see the abbreviation 'ASL' used as well; it stands for 'Above Sea Level'."

(6): 2967. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1111/jvim.15904](https://doi.org/10.1111%2Fjvim.15904). [PMC](/wiki/PMC_(identifier) "PMC (identifier)") [7968404](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7968404). [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [33037851](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33037851). "The horses lived at five different altitudes: 0 masl (n=23), 1000 mamsl (3280 famsl, n=25)..."

(13): 1599–1618. [Bibcode](/wiki/Bibcode_(identifier) "Bibcode (identifier)"):[2006QSRv...25.1599N](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2006QSRv...25.1599N). [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1016/j.quascirev.2005.11.014](https://doi.org/10.1016%2Fj.quascirev.2005.11.014). (11): 2197–2209. [Bibcode](/wiki/Bibcode_(identifier) "Bibcode (identifier)"):[2011JApMC..50.2197M](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2011JApMC..50.2197M). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Height above sea level](/w/index.php?title=Height_above_sea_level&redirect=no "Height above sea level"))

Elevation of a location above a standardized sea level

"AMSL" redirects here. For other uses, see [AMSL (disambiguation)](/wiki/AMSL_(disambiguation) "AMSL (disambiguation)").

**Height above mean sea level** is a measure of a location's [vertical distance](/wiki/Vertical_position "Vertical position") ([height](/wiki/Height "Height"), [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") or [altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude")) in reference to a [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") based on a historic [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level"). In [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy"), it is formalized as [orthometric height](/wiki/Orthometric_height "Orthometric height"). The zero level varies in different countries due to different reference points and historic measurement periods. [Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and [other forces](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence") can cause sea levels and elevations to vary over time.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Height_above_mean_sea_level&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Uses")]

Elevation or altitude above sea level is a standard measurement for:

* [Geographic locations](/wiki/Geographic_location "Geographic location") such as towns, mountains and other [landmarks](/wiki/Landmark "Landmark").
* The top of [buildings](/wiki/Building "Building") and other [structures](/wiki/Nonbuilding_structure "Nonbuilding structure").
* [Mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") infrastructure, particularly underground.
* Flying objects such as [airplanes](/wiki/Airplane "Airplane") or [helicopters](/wiki/Helicopters "Helicopters") below a [transition altitude](/wiki/Flight_level#Transition_altitude "Flight level") defined by local regulations.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Units and abbreviations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Height_above_mean_sea_level&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Units and abbreviations")]

Elevation or altitude is generally expressed as "[metres](/wiki/Metre "Metre") above mean sea level" in the [metric system](/wiki/Metric_system "Metric system"), or "[feet](/wiki/Foot_(length) "Foot (length)") above mean sea level" in [United States customary](/wiki/United_States_customary_units "United States customary units") and [imperial units](/wiki/Imperial_units "Imperial units"). Common abbreviations in English are:

* AMSL – above mean sea level[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* AOD or AODN – above ordnance datum[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* ASL – above sea level[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* FAMSL – feet above mean sea level[[6]](#cite_note-horses-6)
* FASL – feet above sea level[[7]](#cite_note-california-7)
* MAMSL – metres above mean sea level[[6]](#cite_note-horses-6)
* MASL – metres above sea level[[6]](#cite_note-horses-6)[[7]](#cite_note-california-7)
* MSL – mean sea level[[8]](#cite_note-everest-8)

For elevations or altitudes, often just the abbreviation MSL is used, e.g., [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest") (8849 m MSL), or the reference to sea level is omitted completely, e.g., Mount Everest (8849 m).[[8]](#cite_note-everest-8)

## Methods of measurement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Height_above_mean_sea_level&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Methods of measurement")]

[Altimetry](/wiki/Altimetry "Altimetry") is the measurement of altitude or elevation above sea level. Common techniques are:[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

* [Surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying"), especially [levelling](/wiki/Levelling "Levelling").
* [Global Navigation Satellite System](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") (such as [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS")), where a receiver [determines a location](/wiki/Multilateration "Multilateration") from [pseudoranges](/wiki/Pseudorange "Pseudorange") to multiple [satellites](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite"). A [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid") is needed to convert the 3D position to sea-level elevation.
* [Pressure altimeter](/wiki/Pressure_altimeter "Pressure altimeter") measuring [atmospheric pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure"), which decreases as altitude increases. Since atmospheric pressure varies with the weather, too, a recent local measure of the pressure at a known altitude is needed to calibrate the altimeter.
* [Stereoscopy](/wiki/Stereoscopy "Stereoscopy") in [aerial photography](/wiki/Aerial_photography "Aerial photography").
* Aerial [lidar](/wiki/Lidar "Lidar") and [satellite laser altimetry](/wiki/Satellite_laser_altimetry "Satellite laser altimetry").
* Aerial or satellite [radar altimetry](/wiki/Radar_altimeter "Radar altimeter").

Accurate measurement of historical mean sea levels is complex. Land mass [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence") (as occurs naturally in some regions) can give the appearance of [rising sea levels](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise"). Conversely, markings on land masses that are [uplifted](/wiki/Tectonic_uplift "Tectonic uplift") (due to geological processes) can suggest a relative lowering of mean sea level.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
